---
title: "Architecture"
subtitle: "The method, end to end"
---

## The pipeline under study

The agent is organised along **three stages** that turn a frame into an action.
Each stage is evaluated on its own tab.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    subgraph SIM[1. Capture]
        A[Emulator] --> B[Screen capture]
    end
    subgraph DET[2. Perception]
        C[Object detection<br/>YOLOv11] --> D[State restructuring]
    end
    subgraph AI[3. Decision]
        E[RL policy]
    end

    B -->|frame| C
    D -->|state| E
```

At run time the stages form a **closed perception-action loop**: each action
changes the game, producing the next frame, and the change in frames is the
reward signal that trains the policy.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    F[Frame] -->|detection -> state| G[RL policy]
    G -->|action: direction + shoot| H[Action in game]
    H --> I[New frame]
    I -->|reward signal| G
    I --> F
```

## Stages

1. **Capture.** The game runs in an emulator at a fixed resolution; frames are
   grabbed from the window. See [Capture](capture.qmd).
2. **Perception.** A YOLOv11 detector returns bounding boxes, which are
   restructured into a state vector. See [Detection](detection.qmd) and
   [State](state.qmd).
3. **Decision.** A reinforcement-learning policy maps the state to an action
   (movement direction + shoot), injected back into the game. See [RL](rl.qmd)
   and [Inputs](inputs.qmd).

## Materials

| Stage | Method / tooling |
|-------|------------------|
| Capture | LDPlayer 9 emulator, frames via `mss` |
| Perception | YOLOv11 (Ultralytics), trained on the Roboflow `bloxxy/brawl-stars-dataset` (10 classes, MIT) |
| Decision | reinforcement learning (Q-learning / PPO, to be compared) |
| Inputs | ADB (swipe for movement, tap to shoot) |

## Why vision-only

The agent is deliberately restricted to pixels, with no privileged access to
game internals. This keeps the result a fair test of the central hypothesis —
that perception accurate and stable enough to support a learned policy can be
recovered from the screen alone.
